paragraph: LINFIELD-ON-THE-BROOK, VT — The Linfield-on-the-Brook Town Council voted 5-2 on Monday evening to approve a $218,400 contract with Hargrove Municipal Paving for the resurfacing of eleven deteriorated road segments throughout the borough, including the long-contested stretch of Creswell Avenue between Oak Hollow Drive and the firehouse. Council member Dorothea Finch, one of two dissenting votes, argued that the bid process had been too compressed, with only a twelve-day window for contractors to respond. Council Chair Raymond Bellefleur countered that three qualified bids had been received and that a further delay would push the project into the following fiscal year, adding an estimated $14,000 in material cost escalation. Public Works Director Gale Mattson confirmed that crews would begin mobilizing no later than June 9th, weather permitting, and that affected residents on Creswell Avenue would receive forty-eight-hour advance notice before lane closures commenced. The project encompasses approximately 6.4 lane-miles of road surface and is expected to be completed by mid-August. Funding comes from a combination of the town's Capital Improvement Reserve and $87,000 in state Transportation Enhancement Program funds secured in February.

restate_1:
OFFICIAL NOTICE — TOWN OF LINFIELD-ON-THE-BROOK
Road Resurfacing Program — Contract Award Announcement

The Linfield-on-the-Brook Town Council, at its regular Monday evening meeting, voted 5-2 to award a paving and resurfacing contract in the amount of $218,400 to Hargrove Municipal Paving. The contract covers eleven road segments rated in deteriorated condition, including the Creswell Avenue corridor between Oak Hollow Drive and the fire station. Three qualified bids were received and evaluated through the competitive procurement process. Council Chair Raymond Bellefleur noted that deferring award to a subsequent fiscal year would have added an estimated $14,000 in material escalation costs. Work is scheduled to commence no later than June 9th, weather permitting. Residents along Creswell Avenue will receive forty-eight-hour written notice prior to the initiation of lane restrictions. The project encompasses approximately 6.4 lane-miles and carries a projected completion date of mid-August. Funding is provided through the Capital Improvement Reserve and $87,000 in Transportation Enhancement Program funds awarded by the state in February. Questions may be directed to the Public Works Department.
restate_2:
Minutes excerpt — Linfield-on-the-Brook Town Council, Regular Session
Agenda Item 6: Paving Contract Award

Council Chair Bellefleur opened discussion on the proposed contract with Hargrove Municipal Paving for $218,400 covering eleven road segments including Creswell Avenue between Oak Hollow Drive and the firehouse. Member Finch objected, stating the twelve-day bid solicitation window was insufficient for meaningful competition. Chair Bellefleur responded that three qualified bids had been received. Member Finch moved to table; motion failed 2-5. Member Finch and one colleague voted no on the main motion. The main motion to award the contract carried 5-2. Public Works Director Mattson confirmed mobilization by June 9th, weather permitting, with forty-eight-hour notice to Creswell Avenue residents before lane closures. Scope: approximately 6.4 lane-miles. Estimated completion: mid-August. Funding: Capital Improvement Reserve plus $87,000 in state Transportation Enhancement Program funds. Director Mattson further noted that delay into the next fiscal year would have increased material costs by an estimated $14,000. No further public comment. Item closed.
restate_3: I drive Creswell Avenue every single morning to get my kids to school, and I have to say I was relieved when I heard the news Monday night. The council finally voted to fix those roads. Five to two, they approved a contract with Hargrove Municipal Paving for $218,400. Eleven road sections are on the list, and yes, Creswell Avenue between Oak Hollow and the firehouse is included, which is the one everyone has been complaining about for years. I did hear that two council members voted against it because they thought the bids were rushed through in only twelve days. I understand that concern. But the chair said three bids came in anyway, so it is not like nobody competed for the work. If they had waited, they were looking at fourteen thousand dollars more in costs next year. Public Works said they will let us know forty-eight hours before they shut down the lanes. Work starts by June 9th at the latest, and the whole thing should be done by mid-August. Part of the money, $87,000 of it, comes from a state program.
restate_4: Good morning, Linfield-on-the-Brook. This is your weekly road-works update from the mayor's desk. I am pleased to report that after considerable community input and months of planning, the Town Council voted Monday to greenlight a major paving effort across eleven of our most deteriorated road sections. The $218,400 contract goes to Hargrove Municipal Paving, and it includes the Creswell Avenue stretch between Oak Hollow Drive and the firehouse that so many of you have flagged to me and to the council. I want to acknowledge the concerns raised by council member Finch about the length of the bid window. The council weighed those concerns against the practical reality that waiting another full fiscal year would cost taxpayers an additional estimated $14,000. Three competitive bids were received, and the council made its decision. Public Works Director Mattson will ensure Creswell Avenue residents get forty-eight hours of notice before lane closures begin, with a mobilization target of June 9th. The project should be fully wrapped by mid-August. We are combining Capital Improvement Reserve dollars with $87,000 in state Transportation Enhancement Program funds that we secured in February, keeping the local share manageable.

paragraph: PELHAM HOLLOW, OH — The Pelham Hollow School Board approved a $4.1 million renovation plan for Garrison Elementary School at its regular monthly meeting on Thursday, with the measure passing 6-1. The lone dissenting vote came from board member Curtis Alvarado, who requested additional study on whether a phased approach might reduce disruption to the school's 412 enrolled students. The approved plan calls for full replacement of the school's HVAC system, asbestos abatement in the original 1961 wing, and the construction of a new 3,400-square-foot multipurpose room to replace the undersized gymnasium. Superintendent Harriet Kolb told the board that the building had received a D-plus rating in the district's most recent facilities assessment, conducted in October, and that postponing the renovation further risked triggering emergency closures. Construction is slated to begin the day after the academic year concludes on June 13th, with a targeted completion date of August 29th to allow staff setup before the fall semester. The district has secured a $1.6 million low-interest loan through the Ohio Municipal Finance Commission, with the remainder drawn from the district's capital fund balance, which stood at $2.7 million as of March 31st.

PELHAM HOLLOW SCHOOL DISTRICT — FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

The Board of Education voted 6-1 at its regular monthly meeting to approve a $4.1 million renovation of Garrison Elementary School. The project addresses three critical facility needs identified in the October facilities assessment, which rated the building at D-plus: complete replacement of the heating, ventilation, and air conditioning system; asbestos abatement in the wing constructed in 1961; and construction of a 3,400-square-foot multipurpose room to replace the existing undersized gymnasium. Superintendent Harriet Kolb noted that continued deferral of these projects carried a meaningful risk of mandatory emergency closures affecting the school's 412 enrolled students. Construction will begin June 14th, the day following the close of the 2024-2025 academic year, and is scheduled for completion by August 29th. The district has secured financing through a $1.6 million low-interest loan from the Ohio Municipal Finance Commission; the remaining cost will be covered by the district's capital fund balance, which totaled $2.7 million as of March 31st. Board member Curtis Alvarado cast the sole dissenting vote, citing a preference for further study of a phased project approach.

restate_3: My kids both go to Garrison Elementary and honestly I have been worried about that building for a couple of years. The gym is so small that they have to stagger PE classes just to get everyone in, and I remember hearing something about asbestos in the old part of the building. restate_4: Pelham Hollow families, I am writing today with an important update about Garrison Elementary School. After a thorough review process and a 6-1 vote at Thursday's board meeting, our district is moving forward with a $4.1 million renovation that will finally bring this beloved school up to the standards our students deserve. The work covers three priority areas: a full replacement of the heating and cooling system that has become increasingly unreliable, asbestos abatement in the 1961 wing, and the construction of a new 3,400-square-foot multipurpose room that will give students adequate space for physical education and community activities. I want to be transparent with our community: the October facilities assessment gave the building a D-plus rating. Superintendent Kolb was direct with the board that further delay risked triggering emergency closures. We cannot allow that to happen to our students and families. Construction begins June 14th, and we are targeting completion by August 29th so that teachers have time to prepare before fall. We secured a $1.6 million low-interest loan through the Ohio Municipal Finance Commission, with the remainder coming from our capital fund balance of $2.7 million.

paragraph: MILLERTON, NC — The Millerton Chamber of Commerce honored Rosalind Quimby on Saturday evening at the annual Founders Night Banquet at the Overlook Grange Hall, presenting her with the 2025 Community Cornerstone Award for thirty-one years of volunteer service to the town's Meals on Wheels program. Quimby, 68, a retired postal carrier, took over coordination of the program in 1994 when it was serving fourteen homebound residents per week and has since grown it to 127 weekly recipients across four rural delivery routes. Chamber president Gerald Osei described Quimby's contribution as immeasurable, noting that she had personally logged an estimated 14,200 volunteer hours over the course of her tenure. The Founders Night Banquet drew 214 attendees, its largest turnout since 2019. Quimby was nominated by a coalition of seven local organizations, including the Millerton Senior Resource Center and the First Congregational Church of Millerton. In accepting the award, Quimby acknowledged her team of forty-three active volunteers, saying the recognition belonged equally to all of them. The Community Cornerstone Award, established in 1989, is presented annually to a resident whose unpaid civic contributions have materially improved quality of life in the greater Millerton area.

paragraph: DARVALE, MN — Darvale city officials announced on Wednesday that the Darvale Public Library will extend its weekday evening hours beginning June 1st, remaining open until 8:30 p.m. Monday through Thursday, up from the current closing time of 6:00 p.m. The change was made possible by the passage of Referendum 14-B in last November's municipal election, in which 61.4 percent of voters approved a 0.3-mill property tax increase dedicated to library operations. Library Director Pauline Schrader said the additional 2.5 hours of service per evening would allow the library to expand its adult literacy programming, add a second weekly homework-help session for middle and high school students, and accommodate the many working residents who have historically been unable to visit during daytime hours. The Darvale Public Library, housed in the 1927 Carnton Building on Broad Street, currently circulates approximately 38,000 items annually and registers an average of 1,140 patron visits per week. Schrader also confirmed that two new part-time staff positions, budgeted at $34,800 combined annually, would be posted by May 30th to support the expanded schedule.

paragraph: HARWICH CORNERS, MA — The Harwich Corners Select Board voted unanimously on Tuesday to award a $97,500 grant from the Community Spaces Improvement Fund to the Harwich Corners Neighborhood Garden Cooperative for the construction of a twenty-four-plot raised-bed garden on the vacant town-owned parcel at the corner of Elgin Street and Pond Road. The cooperative, founded in 2019 by a group of twelve residents, has operated a smaller temporary garden on borrowed church property for the past four seasons. Cooperative chair Tomas Birkenfeld said the new site, which will also include a tool-storage barn, a composting station, and an ADA-accessible pathway, is expected to be operational by late summer. The board stipulated that at least six of the twenty-four plots must be reserved for low- and moderate-income households at a reduced annual fee of $15, compared to the standard $60 rate. Construction drawings are being finalized by Elms & Hayward Design of nearby Kettleton. Board member Vivian Rourke praised the project as an exemplary model of community-driven land stewardship that transforms an underutilized asset into an active neighborhood amenity at relatively modest cost to taxpayers.

paragraph: BRENTON FALLS, WI — The Brenton Falls Common Council approved a revised municipal water rate schedule on Monday night by a 7-2 margin, setting a 4.8 percent increase in base residential rates effective August 1st. The new schedule raises the monthly base charge for a standard three-quarter-inch residential meter from $18.40 to $19.28 and adjusts tiered consumption rates proportionally. Utility Director Marcus Hedlund told the council that the increase was necessitated by a 22 percent rise in chemical treatment costs since 2022 and the need to fund approximately $340,000 in deferred maintenance at the Clearwater Avenue pumping station. A residential customer using the town median of 4,800 gallons per month will see a monthly bill increase of approximately $3.10. Council members Edna Wolfe and Patrick Throckmorton voted against the measure, both arguing that the increase should be phased over two years to ease the burden on fixed-income residents. Hedlund acknowledged the concern but noted that a phased increase would require borrowing to cover the maintenance gap, ultimately costing ratepayers more. The water utility serves approximately 3,100 residential and 420 commercial accounts in Brenton Falls and the adjacent township of Glenway.

paragraph: COPPERGATE, PA — The Coppergate Borough Council held a public hearing on Thursday for the proposed rezoning of 14.2 acres along the former Dellway Tannery site from Industrial-Heavy to Mixed-Use Development, a step that would allow residential construction of up to 180 units alongside ground-floor retail. The hearing drew sixty-eight residents, a substantially higher turnout than the twelve to twenty typically seen at zoning proceedings. Supporters, including representatives of developer Westmount Residential Partners, emphasized the creation of approximately 240 construction jobs and an estimated $1.9 million in annual property tax revenue once the development reaches full occupancy. Opponents, led by the Coppergate Neighborhood Preservation Coalition, raised concerns about traffic on Dellway Road, the adequacy of the local wastewater system, and the potential loss of the industrial employment base the site once provided. Borough Planning Director Shula Gretz indicated that an updated traffic impact study would be required before the council votes on the rezoning application. A second public hearing has been scheduled for June 26th, with council action expected at the July 14th regular meeting.

paragraph: STILLWOOD, GA — The Stillwood City Commission presented the 2025 Mayor's Award for Civic Excellence to Franklin Oduya at a ceremony held at the Stillwood Municipal Auditorium on Friday evening. Oduya, 54, a local accountant and lifelong Stillwood resident, was recognized for founding and sustaining the Stillwood Youth Financial Literacy Program, which since 2013 has delivered free personal finance education to more than 2,400 students at Stillwood Middle and High Schools. Mayor Constance Aldrich described the program as filling a critical gap in the public school curriculum, noting that students who completed the eight-session course showed measurably higher rates of savings account enrollment upon entering the workforce. The award, accompanied by a commemorative plaque and a $500 donation in the recipient's name to a charity of their choice, has been presented annually since 2001. Oduya directed the donation to the Halberg Memorial Library's summer reading fund. Commission Chair Bernadette Ng noted that this year's nomination process had drawn a record eleven candidates, reflecting what she called an unusually robust landscape of civic volunteerism in the city.

paragraph: KETTLETON, VT — The Kettleton School Board voted 4-3 on Wednesday to adopt a revised school calendar for the 2025-2026 academic year that moves the first day of instruction from September 3rd to August 27th and shifts the last instructional day from June 19th to June 12th. Proponents argued that an earlier start allows more instructional time before the January state assessment window and aligns the end of the school year more closely with regional summer employment and recreation patterns. Opponents, including board members Cecilia Harmon, Douglas Park, and Ora Lindstrom, said the August start conflicted with family vacation schedules and placed undue heat stress on students in the district's two oldest school buildings, which lack air conditioning. Superintendent Gerald Vance told the board that portable cooling units would be rented for the 2025-2026 year at an estimated cost of $22,000 and that a capital request for permanent HVAC installation in the two older buildings would be submitted in the spring budget cycle. The new calendar adds two professional development days and reduces the number of emergency delay days from eight to five.

The majority argued that starting earlier gives teachers more runway before the January state testing window and that finishing earlier aligns better with how families and local employers actually use the summer. The three dissenting board members raised two concrete objections: August openings collide with family vacation plans, and the district's two oldest buildings have no air conditioning, which could make late August attendance an uncomfortable experience for students. Superintendent Gerald Vance said portable cooling units will be brought in at an estimated $22,000 for the coming year and that a formal capital request for permanent HVAC in those buildings will go into the spring budget. paragraph: LOWMERE, TX — Lowmere's Town Council allocated $560,000 from its Infrastructure Renewal Bond fund at Tuesday's regular meeting toward the replacement of the aging Aldercroft Road Bridge, a 68-foot single-span structure that has carried a posted weight limit of five tons since a 2022 inspection identified significant deck deterioration and substructure corrosion. The council voted 6-0 to accept a design-build contract with Conestoga Bridge & Civil of Brackwater, the lowest of three qualified bids. Engineering drawings are expected to be finalized by late July, with construction to begin in September following the harvest season to minimize disruption to the agricultural vehicles that rely heavily on the crossing. The replacement bridge will be designed to a 35-ton load rating and a 75-year service life. During construction, an approved detour route along Fenwick Mill Road will add an estimated 4.2 miles to local trips. Town Engineer Weston Greer noted that the federal Highway Bridge Replacement Program will reimburse 80 percent of eligible costs, reducing the net town contribution to approximately $112,000.

Total estimated cost is $1.23 million. The commission voted 5-0 to advance the plan to the full City Council for funding consideration. Parks Director Lenora Whitfield said the renovation would transform a significantly underutilized asset, noting that the park's current average weekly visitor count of approximately 340 falls well below the citywide average of 680 for parks of comparable size. paragraph: PELHAM HOLLOW, OH — The Pelham Hollow Village Council voted 4-1 at its May meeting to place a 1.4-mill parks levy on the November ballot, which, if approved by voters, would generate approximately $830,000 annually over five years for the acquisition and development of new parkland. Council member Ida Cheswick cast the lone dissenting vote, suggesting the levy amount was excessive given the current property tax burden on residential homeowners. Village Administrator Brent Sokolowski presented data showing that Pelham Hollow has 1.2 acres of parkland per 1,000 residents, significantly below the state-recommended benchmark of 6.25 acres per 1,000. Mayor Diane Forthwright said the levy would allow the village to pursue acquisition of two parcels currently under negotiation: a 9-acre former nursery on Thorburn Road and a 4.5-acre former orchard adjacent to Whitcomb Elementary School. Voters last approved a parks levy in Pelham Hollow in 2009. A community education campaign, to be led by a volunteer committee, will be organized over the summer months in advance of the November election.

paragraph: GRAYVALE, KY — The Grayvale Independent School District Board of Education recognized fourteen educators at its quarterly awards ceremony on Tuesday with the 2025 Distinguished Service in Education Award, the district's highest honor for instructional staff. Among those honored was Marcella Thorn, a fifth-grade teacher at Crestwood Elementary who has taught in the district for 29 years and is credited with developing the district's current reading intervention framework, which has been adopted by three neighboring districts. Board Chair Augusto Marcello said the awards program, established in 1997, had recognized 212 educators over its 28-year history. Nominations are submitted by principals and reviewed by a panel that includes two board members, the superintendent, and two community representatives. This year's field included 41 nominations across the district's eleven schools. Superintendent Colette Ingram noted that the district had seen measurable improvements in third-grade reading proficiency over the past four years, rising from 54 percent at grade level to 71 percent, a trajectory she attributed in part to the intervention model Thorn pioneered. The ceremony was attended by approximately 180 guests including family members, colleagues, and community leaders.

paragraph: DUNMORE CROSSING, ID — The Dunmore Crossing Town Council tabled a decision on a proposed 3.2 percent increase in municipal solid waste collection fees at Monday's meeting, requesting additional financial documentation from the contracting hauler, Cascade Waste Solutions, before proceeding. The proposed increase would raise the monthly residential curbside collection fee from $23.75 to $24.51. Cascade Waste Solutions regional manager Sylvia Holt told the council that rising diesel costs and new state tonnage disposal fees at the Hartfield Regional Landfill were the primary drivers of the requested adjustment. Council member Thaddeus Berridge asked that the company provide a line-item cost breakdown and a copy of the current landfill tipping fee schedule before the council considers approval. A special meeting has been called for June 4th to revisit the fee schedule. The current service contract, signed in 2021, is scheduled to expire in December 2026. Town Administrator Jayne Pollard said the council will likely begin an open solicitation for a new multiyear contract in early 2026, and that the pending fee adjustment would carry through only to the contract's expiration.

paragraph: HARWICH CORNERS, MA — The Harwich Corners Conservation Commission approved on Thursday an Order of Conditions allowing construction of a new footbridge across Sallow Creek in Ridgeway Nature Preserve, subject to a set of twelve environmental stipulations designed to protect the creek's riparian buffer and resident brook trout population. The 28-foot prefabricated timber bridge, funded through a $68,000 Massachusetts Recreational Trails Program grant, will replace a failing wooden plank crossing that was condemned in 2023 following a structural inspection. Commission Chair Audra Morel said the project had been redesigned three times to minimize in-stream work, ultimately settling on a helical pier foundation system that eliminates the need for any in-water equipment operation. Work must be conducted between July 1st and September 15th, outside the brook trout spawning window. The Ridgeway Nature Preserve, managed by the town in partnership with the Harwich Corners Land Trust, encompasses 214 acres of forest, wetland, and meadow habitat and draws an estimated 18,000 visitor-trips annually. The bridge is expected to be installed within a single five-day construction window.

paragraph: MILLERTON, NC — The Millerton Town Council approved its fiscal year 2026 operating budget on second reading at a special session held Thursday evening, adopting a $9.84 million spending plan that holds the property tax rate flat at $0.42 per $100 of assessed value. The budget passed 4-1, with council member Bart Fenwick dissenting due to what he characterized as insufficient investment in code enforcement staffing. The approved budget includes a 3.1 percent cost-of-living adjustment for all municipal employees, a $275,000 allocation for replacement of the fire department's 2007 tanker truck, and $118,000 for street light conversion to LED fixtures on the downtown corridor. General fund reserves are projected to end the fiscal year at 18.3 percent of expenditures, above the council's adopted target of 15 percent. Finance Director Genevieve Achebe noted that steady growth in local sales tax receipts, up 6.2 percent year-over-year, had provided additional budget flexibility. The new fiscal year begins July 1st. The budget document is available for public inspection at the Millerton Town Hall and on the town's website.

paragraph: DARVALE, MN — The Darvale Township Board of Supervisors voted 3-0 on Wednesday to reject a conditional use permit application from Ironvale Aggregate, LLC, which had sought permission to open a gravel extraction operation on 78 acres of agricultural land along County Road 19. The board found that Ironvale's submitted traffic impact study failed to adequately account for heavy vehicle loads on County Road 19 during spring load restrictions, and that the application's noise modeling did not reflect actual topographic conditions adjacent to the proposed blast zone. Three neighboring property owners had filed formal objections. Board Chair Odette Klemm said the denial was without prejudice and that the applicant could resubmit with a revised application addressing the board's stated deficiencies. paragraph: BRENTON FALLS, WI — Brenton Falls Elementary School was named a 2025 Shining Star School by the Wisconsin Community Schools Coalition at a regional recognition luncheon held in Lakeridge on Friday. The award, given annually to schools demonstrating exceptional integration of community partnerships into the educational program, cited Brenton Falls Elementary's collaboration with the Brenton Falls Senior Center, through which forty-three senior volunteers contribute a combined 1,200 hours per school year to tutoring, classroom reading assistance, and after-school enrichment. Principal Margot Deevers accepted the award on behalf of the school's staff and community partners. The coalition recognized eleven schools statewide from a field of thirty-four nominees. Deevers credited the school's community partnership coordinator, Raul Escobar, who joined the staff in 2021 and has dramatically expanded the scope of the senior volunteer program, as well as partnerships with the Brenton Falls Public Library, two local agricultural operations, and the Brenton Falls Fire Department. The school serves 334 students in kindergarten through fifth grade.

paragraph: COPPERGATE, PA — The Coppergate Borough Council voted 5-2 on Tuesday to amend the local zoning ordinance to permit accessory dwelling units by right on all single-family residential parcels of 7,500 square feet or greater, removing the requirement for a special exception hearing that had previously added an average of four months and $2,200 in legal fees to the approval process. The amendment caps accessory dwelling unit floor area at 850 square feet and prohibits exterior stairways visible from the primary street frontage. Borough Solicitor Fiona Brandt confirmed that the change aligns Coppergate's code with the state's 2024 housing access legislation. Council members Theodore Stroud and Agnes Harkins voted against the measure, both citing concerns about the precedent of reducing neighbor notification requirements. The planning commission had recommended approval by a 4-1 vote in April. Borough Manager Laird Hutchinson estimated that the simplified process could result in 15 to 25 additional accessory dwelling unit applications per year, which would modestly expand the borough's housing supply without requiring infrastructure investment.

paragraph: STILLWOOD, GA — The Stillwood City Commission approved a $1.4 million contract on Monday for the construction of a new pedestrian and bicycle pathway connecting the Riverton Greenway to the Stillwood Central Business District, a 1.1-mile route that will close the most significant remaining gap in the city's non-motorized transportation network. The project was awarded to Clearpath Construction, which submitted the lowest of four responsive bids. The pathway will run along the disused Westing Rail corridor, with the city having secured a rails-to-trails easement from the property owner in 2023. Construction is expected to begin in August and be complete by March 2026. The project is funded through a combination of a $980,000 federal Transportation Alternatives Program grant and $420,000 from the city's capital improvement budget. Commissioner Raylene Osei, chair of the transportation committee, noted that the pathway is projected to serve an estimated 1,500 daily users once connected to the existing Riverton Greenway, which currently draws approximately 4,200 user-trips per week at its nearest terminus point.

paragraph: ARDENMOOR, OR — The Ardenmoor City Council on Tuesday gave initial approval to an ordinance establishing a historic preservation overlay district encompassing twenty-six properties in the city's Northgate neighborhood, most of them residential structures built between 1895 and 1940. The ordinance, which will require a second reading and public hearing before final adoption, would mandate review by a five-member Historic Preservation Commission before any demolition, significant exterior alteration, or new construction within the overlay zone. Supporters, including the Ardenmoor Heritage Society, said the overlay would protect an irreplaceable architectural legacy in what remains one of the city's most intact early-twentieth-century neighborhoods. Several property owners within the proposed district spoke in opposition, expressing concern about potential restrictions on their ability to maintain or modify their homes. City Attorney Winifred Okello clarified that the overlay would not prevent maintenance, painting, or interior alterations. A final vote is scheduled for June 17th. The city's current historic preservation program designates eleven individual landmarks but has no neighborhood-level overlay districts.

A second reading and public hearing are required before the ordinance becomes law, with a final vote set for June 17th. If adopted, the overlay would require anyone seeking to demolish, substantially alter the exterior of, or build new within the zone to first go through a review by a five-member Historic Preservation Commission. The Ardenmoor Heritage Society supported the measure, arguing the neighborhood is one of the city's most architecturally intact early twentieth-century areas. A handful of property owners within the proposed district spoke against it, worried about restrictions on what they can do with their own homes. City Attorney Winifred Okello drew an important line: the overlay does not touch maintenance, painting, or anything inside the house. Ardenmoor currently designates eleven individual landmarks but has no district-level protections.

paragraph: LINFIELD-ON-THE-BROOK, VT — The Linfield-on-the-Brook Board of Civil Authority certified on Monday the results of the May 6th local election, confirming that incumbent Town Treasurer Elspeth Garland was re-elected to a third four-year term with 68.3 percent of the vote, defeating challenger Rowan Kieffer, who received the remaining 31.7 percent of ballots cast. Voter turnout was 38.4 percent of registered voters, or 1,247 of 3,248 registered, the highest turnout for a non-presidential local election in Linfield-on-the-Brook since 2013. Also certified was the passage of Article 7, which authorizes the selectboard to borrow up to $450,000 for bridge repair on Orchard Gap Road. Article 7 passed 714-512. Articles 1 through 6, comprising routine appropriations, passed with margins ranging from 79 to 88 percent. Garland, a certified public accountant who has overseen the town's finances since 2017, said she would focus her third term on completing the transition to a new cloud-based financial management system and on retiring the remaining balance of a 2016 equipment bond ahead of schedule.

Incumbent Treasurer Elspeth Garland won a third four-year term with 68.3 percent of the vote, with challenger Rowan Kieffer receiving 31.7 percent. Garland, a certified public accountant who has run the town's finances since 2017, said her priorities for the new term include finishing the move to a cloud-based financial management system and paying off the remaining balance of a 2016 equipment bond before it is due. Voters also approved Article 7, which gives the selectboard authority to borrow up to $450,000 for bridge work on Orchard Gap Road. That measure passed 714-512. The other six articles, covering routine spending authorizations, sailed through with support ranging from 79 to 88 percent.

That bridge needs work, so I am glad it went through even though it was not a blowout. The routine articles, one through six, all passed easily between 79 and 88 percent. Garland said she wants to finish moving the town's finances onto a cloud system and pay off the 2016 bond early. Sounds good to me.

Elspeth has served this community with skill and integrity since 2017, and the voters clearly recognize it. Perhaps equally noteworthy is that 1,247 of our 3,248 registered voters, 38.4 percent, came out to vote. The remaining articles passed with strong margins. Congratulations to Elspeth and thank you to everyone who voted.

paragraph: KETTLETON, VT — Kettleton's Planning and Zoning Commission voted 4-1 on Thursday to recommend approval of a proposed 44-unit workforce housing development on the 5.8-acre former Kettleton Creamery site on Foundry Road. The project, submitted by Northpine Housing Partners, would consist of 28 two-bedroom and 16 one-bedroom rental units, with 20 percent of units restricted to households earning at or below 80 percent of the area median income. Commissioner Hideo Tanaka cast the lone dissenting vote, citing concerns about the adequacy of on-site parking at the proposed 1.2 spaces per unit ratio. The commission's recommendation now goes to the full Selectboard, which is expected to act at its June 10th meeting. Northpine Housing Partners has indicated it will seek state affordable housing tax credits to finance the income-restricted units and anticipates a construction start in spring 2026. The Kettleton Planning Director, Faye Sorrento, noted that the project would bring the town's housing vacancy rate, currently estimated at 1.4 percent, closer to the 4 percent threshold generally considered indicative of a healthy rental market.

paragraph: LOWMERE, TX — The Lowmere Independent School District held a ribbon-cutting ceremony on Saturday morning for the newly renovated Halberg Memorial Library at Lowmere Preparatory School, following a $610,000 renovation funded through the district's 2023 facility bond. The renovation transformed the library's 4,200-square-foot main floor, replacing fixed shelving with flexible furniture, installing a new maker space with three 3-D printers and recording equipment, and adding a glass-enclosed quiet study room for eighteen students. Library Media Specialist Constance Ferrara said circulation had already increased 34 percent in the two weeks since the soft reopening in late April, compared to the same period last year. Superintendent Lorena Dubois credited the project architect, Fielding & Morse Design Group of nearby Bascom, with preserving the library's 1958 facade and terrazzo floors while completely modernizing the interior. The library is named for Evander Halberg, a longtime district administrator who championed library funding during his twenty-two-year tenure. Approximately 160 guests attended the ceremony, including current students, alumni, and Halberg's surviving daughter, Norma Halberg-Pryce.

paragraph: GRAYVALE, KY — The Grayvale City Council approved an expanded noxious weed control program on Monday, allocating $42,000 to hire two seasonal weed control technicians and purchase herbicide and application equipment for a citywide invasive plant management effort. The program, modeled on a similar initiative in neighboring Aldworth Township, will prioritize treatment of Japanese knotweed, multiflora rose, and autumn olive along public rights-of-way, utility corridors, and city-owned open space parcels. City Arborist Penelope Vance told the council that invasive plant infestations had been documented on approximately 380 acres of city-owned land, and that uncontrolled spread was degrading native plant communities in Grayvale's two nature preserves. Council member Simone Kraft asked whether a cost-share program might be established to help residential property owners with treatment on adjacent private land. City Manager Theodore Bourne said staff would research options and report back by September. The council also voted to apply for a $15,000 state invasive species management grant, which could partially offset program costs in subsequent years. Work is scheduled to begin June 15th.

paragraph: DUNMORE CROSSING, ID — The Dunmore Crossing Historic Preservation Board voted unanimously on Tuesday to recommend that the former Meridian Hotel building at 14 Commerce Street be listed on the Dunmore Crossing Local Register of Historic Places, a designation that would make exterior alterations subject to board review and could qualify the owner for a state historic rehabilitation tax credit of up to 25 percent of eligible project costs. The four-story brick building, constructed in 1903, was the town's primary hostelry for nearly six decades and is one of only three remaining examples of Beaux-Arts commercial architecture in the county. Current owner Clementine Haas, who purchased the building in 2022 and has been working to rehabilitate its upper floors as residential apartments, testified in favor of the designation. The Board's recommendation goes to the Town Council, which must hold a public hearing before acting. Council Chair Avery Johansson indicated that the hearing would be scheduled at the June 3rd council meeting. A local register currently includes thirty-one individual properties and one historic district.

paragraph: MILLERTON, NC — The Millerton Recreation Department reported on Wednesday that registration for the 2025 summer recreation program had reached 418 participants as of May 14th, a 22 percent increase over the 342 enrolled at the same point in 2024 and the highest pre-season enrollment in the program's twelve-year history. Recreation Director Alana McBride attributed the increase to the introduction of three new program tracks this year: a STEM exploration camp, a culinary arts program, and an outdoor adventure series in partnership with the Millerton Land Conservancy. The eight-week program, running June 23rd through August 15th, will be headquartered at the Millerton Community Center on Elm Street and will use Troutdale Park for outdoor programming. McBride said the department had added twelve counselor-in-training slots for high school students and was recruiting four additional part-time program staff to accommodate the expanded enrollment. The department set a capacity cap of 475 participants this summer. Families not yet registered were encouraged to submit applications by June 1st, after which wait-list status would apply.

paragraph: PELHAM HOLLOW, OH — The Pelham Hollow Village Council received a final report Tuesday evening from the Ad Hoc Pothole and Pavement Advisory Committee, which found that the village's residential street network included 247 lane-miles rated in poor or very poor condition out of a total assessed 614 lane-miles, representing a pavement quality deficit estimated to require $6.8 million to fully address over the next decade. The committee, appointed in September and chaired by retired civil engineer Bruno Pfeifer, conducted visual inspections of 183 street segments and reviewed the village's ten-year capital plan. Pfeifer presented the committee's primary recommendation that the village adopt a systematic asset management approach, allocating no less than $600,000 annually to preventive maintenance and targeted resurfacing rather than continuing the current reactive pothole-filling approach, which the committee estimated costs the village 2.3 times more per lane-mile over a ten-year horizon. Council members thanked the committee for its thoroughness and referred the report to the Finance Committee for incorporation into the fiscal year 2026 budget discussions. The council also voted to post the full report on the village website for public review.

paragraph: HARWICH CORNERS, MA — The Harwich Corners Public Schools announced Thursday that Westbrook Middle School Principal Darnell Okafor has been selected as the 2025 Massachusetts Rural Schools Association's Administrator of the Year, an honor chosen by a peer panel of school leaders from across the state's rural districts. Okafor, who has led Westbrook Middle since 2018, was recognized for implementing a comprehensive student support framework that reduced the school's chronic absenteeism rate from 21.4 percent in 2018-2019 to 8.7 percent in 2023-2024 and increased the proportion of students meeting or exceeding expectations on state English language arts assessments from 48 percent to 67 percent over the same period. Superintendent Marlene Fuchs said Okafor's leadership had transformed the culture of the 520-student school and set a standard for the district. Okafor said he dedicated the award to the school's teachers and to the community organizations that partnered with the school to address barriers to student attendance and achievement. He will be formally recognized at the Rural Schools Association's annual conference in Crestfield in October.

paragraph: ARDENMOOR, OR — The Ardenmoor Unified School District School Board voted 6-0 on Wednesday to adopt a new district-wide grading policy, effective with the 2025-2026 academic year, that eliminates grades below 50 percent for work that is submitted and requires teachers to allow students one opportunity to retake or revise any major assessment within ten school days of the original due date. The policy was developed over eighteen months by a teacher and administrator working group and went through two rounds of community input totaling 614 survey responses and four public forums. Assistant Superintendent for Curriculum Dorinda Pham said the policy aligns Ardenmoor with research indicating that grades below 50 percent for submitted work do not accurately reflect student learning and make academic recovery mathematically improbable within a single grading term. Two board members had initially sought a pilot program in selected schools rather than a district-wide rollout, but the working group's data from two early-adopter schools within the district, which showed no significant change in student work completion rates, persuaded the full board to move forward with system-wide implementation.

paragraph: DARVALE, MN — The Darvale City Council approved a $184,000 agreement on Monday with the Darvale Downtown Development Authority for facade improvement grants to be distributed to commercial property owners along the three-block stretch of Main Street between Ridgeway Avenue and Glenmore Court. The grant program, funded through the city's Tax Increment Financing District No. 3 reserve, will provide matching grants of up to $15,000 per storefront for exterior painting, window replacement, signage, and accessible entry improvements. Development Authority Executive Director Cora Aaberg said the program would accept applications beginning June 16th and that staff expected to fund between fourteen and eighteen projects in the first round. The corridor includes 26 commercial buildings, of which 19 are currently owner-occupied. A similar program conducted in the adjacent Tax Increment District No. 1 in 2018 was credited with catalyzing $2.1 million in additional private investment over the following three years. A similar program conducted in the adjacent Tax Increment District No. 1 in 2018 was credited with catalyzing $2.1 million in additional private investment over the following three years. The council also directed staff to develop design guidelines for the Main Street corridor to ensure that grant-funded improvements are consistent with the city's downtown master plan adopted in 2021.

paragraph: COPPERGATE, PA — The Coppergate Volunteer Fire Company celebrated its centennial anniversary on Saturday with a community day at the Coppergate Fire Station on Chestnut Hill Road that drew an estimated 1,400 attendees over the course of eight hours. The event featured apparatus demonstrations, a public open house of both station bays, a historical exhibit assembled by the Coppergate Historical Society documenting the company's founding in 1925, and a chicken barbecue prepared by the Ladies Auxiliary. Mayor Philippa Strome presented the fire company with a ceremonial proclamation and a framed copy of the original 1925 incorporation certificate. Fire Chief Randolph Cass thanked the community for its continued support, noting that the company's annual fund drive has met its goal for seventeen consecutive years and that active membership currently stands at 41, its highest in a decade. Borough Council President Yusuf Ibrahimović called the volunteer fire company one of the borough's most vital and enduring institutions. A commemorative history booklet, written by local historian Francine Woolsey, was distributed to visitors throughout the day.

paragraph: GRAYVALE, KY — The Grayvale Independent School District announced Wednesday that it had received a $320,000 grant from the Kentfield Foundation to launch a three-year pilot of the district's Pathways to Trades program, a structured career and technical education initiative that will provide students in grades ten through twelve with industry-recognized credentials in welding, HVAC technology, and electrical systems through partnerships with three local employers. The participating employers, Grayvale Fabrication Works, Central Kentucky Climate Systems, and Doyle Electrical Contractors, have committed to offering paid summer internships to a combined thirty program graduates annually and to giving hiring preference to completers who apply for entry-level positions. Superintendent Colette Ingram said the program responds directly to documented workforce shortages in the trades that local employers have raised repeatedly with the district. The program will be housed at Grayvale Vocational Center and taught by a combination of licensed CTE teachers and registered journeyworkers under a dual-instructor model. An advisory board including employer representatives, union members, and community college administrators will guide the program's development.

paragraph: BRENTON FALLS, WI — The Brenton Falls Common Council voted 8-1 on Tuesday to establish a food truck licensing program, adopting a new chapter of the municipal code that creates a food truck vendor license, sets a $175 annual fee, requires proof of state food handler permits and liability insurance of no less than $500,000, and designates six approved vending zones throughout the city, including the riverfront plaza and the parking lot adjacent to Calloway Community Park. The single dissenting vote came from council member Lorraine Hedges, who said the program should include a setback requirement to protect brick-and-mortar restaurants. A setback provision had been included in an earlier draft but was removed after the business association argued that the proposed 300-foot rule would have excluded the riverfront zone. Council member Denis Wrzesniewski noted that six to eight food truck operators in Brenton Falls were currently operating without a formal city license due to a gap in the existing code. The new ordinance takes effect August 1st, with a sixty-day grace period for existing operators to come into compliance.

paragraph: LINFIELD-ON-THE-BROOK, VT — The Linfield-on-the-Brook Selectboard unanimously approved on Wednesday an updated emergency operations plan incorporating lessons learned from the August 2023 flood event that inundated forty-seven properties along Creswell Creek and required evacuation of 136 residents. The revised plan includes a new Community Emergency Response Team training program targeting twenty volunteer members to be trained annually, updated flood warning protocols that reduce the activation threshold on the creek's two stream gauges, a mutual aid agreement with the neighboring town of Alderton that formalizes resource-sharing for shelter and equipment, and a pre-identified contract with Keystone Emergency Services for debris removal mobilization within twenty-four hours of a declared emergency. Emergency Management Coordinator Patricia Doyle said the 2023 event exposed specific procedural gaps, particularly in the coordination between the town's public works department and the county emergency management office during the first six hours of the event. The selectboard also voted to establish a capital reserve fund of $30,000 annually to support emergency preparedness equipment purchases and training costs.

paragraph: KETTLETON, VT — The Kettleton Public Library Board of Trustees announced Thursday that the library's Seed Lending Library, launched as a six-month pilot in March 2024, has been made a permanent program following a successful first year in which 312 patron households checked out seed packets representing 84 distinct vegetable and herb varieties. The program allows any library card holder to borrow seed packets each spring and is asked, though not required, to return a portion of seeds saved from the resulting harvest each fall to replenish the collection. Library Director Amara Osei said the seed library has become one of the library's most popular non-book programs, drawing new card holders and generating significant community interest in food gardening. The collection was initially stocked with donated seeds from the Kettleton Community Garden Cooperative and from a $1,400 donation from the Kettleton Garden Club. Following the pilot year, the trustees approved a $600 annual budget line to purchase commercial seed varieties that supplement the community-donated collection. The seed library operates out of a dedicated cabinet adjacent to the periodicals section and is accessible during all library hours.

paragraph: STILLWOOD, GA — The Stillwood City Commission voted 4-1 on Monday to approve a three-year lease agreement with the Stillwood Farmers Market Association, allowing the association to operate a year-round Saturday farmers market on the city-owned Liberty Square plaza beginning July 5th. The new arrangement expands on a temporary seasonal permit that has governed the market's operation since 2017. Under the lease, the association will pay the city $1,200 annually, assume responsibility for vendor coordination, sanitation, and insurance, and maintain a minimum of twelve active vendors on any given market day. Commissioner Avery Pound cast the lone dissenting vote, arguing that the lease rate was below fair market value for use of the plaza. Association President Helene Brackman said the market currently draws approximately 2,200 shoppers on peak Saturdays and generates a combined estimated $1.1 million annually in direct vendor sales. The year-round expansion, previously limited by the temporary permit to June through October, will allow winter markets in November through April, which Brackman said would feature local meat, dairy, preserved foods, and crafts vendors. The city retains the right to reclaim the plaza space for civic events with thirty days' written notice.

Under the arrangement, the association pays the city $1,200 a year, takes on responsibility for vendors, sanitation, and insurance, and must keep at least twelve vendors active on any market day. Commissioner Avery Pound voted no, arguing the city should be charging market value for plaza use. Association President Helene Brackman pointed to the market's impact: about 2,200 shoppers on busy Saturdays and approximately $1.1 million in annual vendor sales. The most significant change is the extension to year-round operation. The old seasonal permit ran June through October. Now November through April will also have markets, with meat, dairy, preserved goods, and crafts filling vendor slots during the quieter winter season. The city can still reclaim the space for civic events with thirty days of written notice.

paragraph: PELHAM HOLLOW, OH — The Pelham Hollow Village Planning Commission held a preliminary review on Tuesday of a site plan submitted by Whitmore Medical Group for a new 8,400-square-foot outpatient clinic on a two-acre parcel at the corner of Fulton Street and Alderton Road. The proposed clinic would house four primary care examination rooms, two urgent care bays, imaging services, and a pharmacy. The commission raised questions about the site's stormwater management plan, which was deemed insufficient for a 100-year storm event under the village's revised stormwater ordinance adopted last year. Commission Chair Ingrid Holloway also requested that the applicant provide a traffic circulation study accounting for the projected 140 daily vehicle trips the facility is expected to generate. Whitmore Medical Group's project engineer, Victor Haines, indicated that a revised stormwater plan and traffic analysis could be submitted within six weeks. A formal public hearing will not be scheduled until the technical review items are resolved. If approved, the clinic would be the first new medical facility constructed in Pelham Hollow in eleven years and is projected to employ twenty-two full-time and part-time staff.

paragraph: DUNMORE CROSSING, ID — The Dunmore Crossing School District announced Wednesday that it will close the West Campus Annex, a standalone four-classroom building that has served as an overflow facility for the district's special education program, at the end of the current school year. Enrollment in the program served by the annex has declined from 62 students in 2020 to 41 in the current year, and the district's facilities audit conducted last fall identified the 1974 structure as the lowest-rated building in the district's portfolio, with a Facilities Condition Index of 72.4, indicating significant deferred maintenance costs. Superintendent Tricia Dunwell said all current West Campus Annex students and staff will be accommodated at the district's two main campuses beginning in September, with program adjustments to be communicated to families by June 16th. The annex building, on leased land, will be returned to the property owner. The closure will produce net operational savings of approximately $88,000 annually, which the district plans to redirect to classroom instructional support. Three staff members currently assigned exclusively to the annex will be offered comparable positions at the main campuses.

paragraph: LOWMERE, TX — Mayor Carla Boudreaux announced Thursday that Lowmere has been selected as one of twelve municipalities in the state to participate in the Texas Municipal League's Small Town Resilience Initiative, a two-year technical assistance program providing each selected town with up to $75,000 in planning, engineering, and legal consulting services to help address critical infrastructure vulnerabilities. Lowmere's application, submitted in February, was supported by documentation of six flood events in the past decade that caused a combined estimated $4.2 million in public and private property damage. The initiative will fund a comprehensive stormwater infrastructure assessment, development of a hazard mitigation plan update, and a drainage improvement feasibility study for the Creekside and Meadow Bluff neighborhoods, which have experienced the most severe and frequent flooding. Town Administrator Denise Garza said the selection represents a significant opportunity for the town, which would not otherwise be able to afford the level of professional technical assistance the program provides. Lowmere's participation begins June 1st, with deliverables expected by May 2027.

paragraph: HARWICH CORNERS, MA — The Harwich Corners Elementary School Parent-Teacher Organization raised $62,400 through its annual Spring Auction and Gala held last Saturday at the Tidewater Club, surpassing its $55,000 goal by 13.4 percent and setting a new single-event fundraising record for the organization. The event drew 188 guests and featured 74 live and silent auction items donated by local businesses, families, and community organizations. PTO President Sofie Andriessen said the funds will be used to purchase updated classroom technology, including 48 new laptops for the school's third and fourth grade classrooms, to fund the school's full year of enrichment field trips at no charge to families, and to replenish the student activity fund, which supports participation in extracurricular programs for students whose families face financial hardship. The PTO acknowledged the lead sponsorship of Harwich Corners Savings Bank and the in-kind printing contributions of Beacon Press & Design. The spring auction has been a cornerstone of the PTO's fundraising program since 1997. Families not yet registered were encouraged to submit applications by June 1st, after which wait-list status would apply.

paragraph: ARDENMOOR, OR — The Ardenmoor City Council voted 5-2 on Monday to accept a $2.3 million federal Community Development Block Grant for infrastructure improvements in the Dawson Flats neighborhood, the city's lowest-income census tract, where 68 percent of households fall at or below 80 percent of the area median income. The grant will fund replacement of 1.8 miles of deteriorated water main, storm sewer improvements on four block segments experiencing chronic basement backups, and resurfacing of six residential streets that have not been improved since the late 1980s. Community Development Director Marguerite Fenn said the city had applied for the grant three times over seven years, and that the award reflects both the sustained effort and improved application quality developed over successive cycles. The two dissenting votes came from council members who questioned the adequacy of the city's local match contribution, set at $415,000. Construction is expected to begin in spring 2026 following completion of the design and permitting phase. The project is expected to benefit approximately 840 Dawson Flats households.

paragraph: BRENTON FALLS, WI — The Brenton Falls Common Council named Wilhelmina Stockton as the recipient of the 2025 Citizen of the Year Award at a ceremony at City Hall on Monday evening. Stockton, 62, a retired schoolteacher, was recognized for her decade-long leadership of the Brenton Falls Literacy Coalition, which she co-founded in 2015 and has since grown into a network of forty-two trained volunteer tutors serving an average of 116 adult learners per year across six community sites including the public library, the community center, and two churches. The Citizen of the Year Award, presented annually since 1978, is selected by a fifteen-member volunteer panel drawn from civic, business, nonprofit, and faith community sectors. Mayor Rosemary Calvetti presented the award plaque, which includes a portrait of the recipient commissioned from Ardenmoor-based artist Dale Prichard. The award plaque includes a portrait of the recipient commissioned from Ardenmoor-based artist Dale Prichard. Stockton told those assembled that the coalition's work was made possible by the generosity of more than 200 individual donors and by the steady institutional support of the Brenton Falls Area Community Foundation, which has provided $124,000 in grants to the coalition since 2016.

paragraph: MILLERTON, NC — The Millerton Town Council held a first reading on Tuesday of a proposed ordinance that would require all new commercial construction of 5,000 square feet or more to include electric vehicle charging infrastructure sized to accommodate at least five percent of total parking spaces, with conduit roughed in for an additional ten percent to allow future expansion at lower cost. The proposal was developed by the town's Sustainability Advisory Committee, which has been meeting monthly since February 2023. Council member Francine Weatherly expressed support but asked that staff clarify whether the requirement would apply to interior parking structures as well as surface lots. Town Attorney Gerardus Boone confirmed that the ordinance as drafted applies to both. Council member Ellsworth Dade asked for data on how similar requirements in comparable municipalities had affected development costs, and the sustainability committee chair, Rosanne Fisk, offered to compile a summary from three peer municipalities that have enacted similar requirements. A public hearing on the ordinance is scheduled for the June 17th council meeting, with a final vote expected in July.

paragraph: COPPERGATE, PA — The Coppergate School District received word this week that it has been awarded a $480,000 grant from the Keystone Foundation for the installation of solar arrays on the rooftops of Coppergate Middle School and the district's central administrative building. The arrays, with a combined capacity of 214 kilowatts, are projected to offset approximately 38 percent of the two buildings' annual electricity consumption, generating an estimated $41,000 in annual utility savings once operational. Superintendent Margaux Deleon said the project also includes an educational monitoring dashboard that will allow students and teachers to track real-time energy generation data and incorporate the system into STEM curriculum. The grant covers 100 percent of installation costs; the district will be responsible for ongoing maintenance estimated at $3,800 annually. Installation is expected to be completed by November, allowing the district to document a full year of generation data before the grant reporting period ends. The district has an existing small solar installation at Coppergate High School, installed in 2018, which has consistently outperformed its projected generation estimates.

paragraph: DUNMORE CROSSING, ID — The Dunmore Crossing Town Council voted 5-0 on Tuesday to approve an agreement with the Dunmore Crossing Arts Alliance for a two-year public art program that will install ten temporary outdoor sculptures at locations throughout the downtown core and the Riverside Trail corridor. The town will contribute $24,000 per year to fund artist stipends, installation, and insurance, while the Arts Alliance will manage the competitive selection process and coordinate logistics. Executive Director Naomi Ostrowski said the program is modeled on a successful initiative in the nearby city of Aldgate and will invite proposals from Idaho artists with preference for work that engages with the region's agricultural and natural heritage. A selection jury will include two council members, two Arts Alliance board members, and one independent arts professional from outside Dunmore Crossing. The first cohort of sculptures is targeted for installation by September 1st, timed to coincide with the Dunmore Crossing Harvest Festival. Council Chair Avery Johansson said the program reflects the council's commitment to making the town's public spaces more dynamic and welcoming while supporting local and regional creative talent.

paragraph: LINFIELD-ON-THE-BROOK, VT — The Linfield-on-the-Brook Selectboard voted 4-1 on Monday to authorize an agreement with the Linfield Senior Services Collaborative for the operation of a weekday senior transportation program using a town-owned accessible van. The program, which will offer door-to-door transportation for qualifying residents aged 65 or older or those with a qualifying disability, will serve an estimated 60 to 80 unduplicated riders in its first year, targeting medical appointments, grocery shopping, and connections to the regional transit hub. The collaborative will staff and manage operations while the town provides the vehicle and covers fuel and maintenance costs estimated at $18,400 annually. The lone dissenting vote came from selectboard member Clement Bourgeois, who preferred contracting the service entirely to a regional transit provider rather than establishing a town-vehicle program. Collaborative Director Bev Halpin said the program fills a specific gap for residents whose needs are not well served by the regional fixed-route transit system, which does not operate on weekends and has no stops within walking distance of several rural residential areas where elderly residents are concentrated.

paragraph: GRAYVALE, KY — The Grayvale City Council took public comment Tuesday evening on a proposed revision to the city's sign ordinance that would impose new restrictions on digital changeable-copy signs along the Route 11 commercial corridor, limiting brightness levels, transition intervals, and the number of such signs permitted per block face. The proposed revision came in response to petitions signed by 318 residents and twelve businesses in the corridor's residential-adjacent blocks, citing driver distraction, light intrusion into adjacent homes, and what petitioners described as a degraded sense of place along what was once a historic commercial streetscape. The International Sign Association submitted written comments arguing that the proposed 0.3-second minimum message duration requirement was more restrictive than standards adopted by any comparable municipality in the state. Council member Fernanda Wicks said she wanted to find a workable middle ground that protects residents without placing excessive compliance burdens on small business owners. A revised draft incorporating feedback from Tuesday's hearing will be presented at the June 24th meeting, with a vote anticipated in July.

A voice from the sign industry also weighed in. The International Sign Association submitted written comments arguing that the proposed 0.3-second minimum message duration is stricter than any comparable municipality in the state has adopted. Council member Fernanda Wicks acknowledged the tension and said the goal is a compromise that genuinely protects residents without becoming an undue burden on small business owners who depend on signage. paragraph: STILLWOOD, GA — The Stillwood City Commission approved on Monday a revised agreement with Pelican Waste Solutions extending the city's solid waste collection contract by three years through December 2028, with a 5.5 percent rate increase effective July 1st. The extension was negotiated without a competitive bid process under a provision in the existing contract allowing one renewal term, a choice city staff said reflects the satisfactory performance record Pelican has established since the contract began in 2021 and the significant staff time that a competitive procurement would require. The residential monthly collection rate will rise from $31.10 to $32.81, including curbside recycling. Commissioner Winston Okello asked that the renewed contract include a performance incentive structure tied to recycling contamination rates, which have been above the regional average in Stillwood's service area. City Manager Frank Augustino confirmed that a contamination penalty clause had been added, providing for a credit to the city of up to $8,000 annually if contamination rates exceed 18 percent. The city has approximately 6,400 residential solid waste accounts.

paragraph: KETTLETON, VT — The Kettleton Town Meeting approved a $240,000 allocation by a show-of-hands vote of 312 to 87 on Saturday morning toward the construction of a covered outdoor pavilion at Founders Memorial Park, a project that has been discussed by the Selectboard for nearly six years. The pavilion, designed by local architect Sybil Cranshaw, will measure 48 by 60 feet, seat up to 220 people under cover, include electrical service, a small equipment storage room, and four accessible restrooms, and be sited to avoid the park's two dozen mature sugar maples. Recreation Committee Chair Owen Lefebvre said the pavilion would allow the park to host the weekly summer concert series, the Heritage Day festival, and other community events regardless of weather for the first time, potentially extending the annual concert series by three to four dates. Total annual attendance at Founders Memorial Park events in 2024 was estimated at 11,400 visitors. Construction bids will be solicited in June, with a groundbreaking targeted for September. The project is expected to be complete by the following spring, in time for the 2026 outdoor season.

Critically, Sybil designed the structure to protect every one of the park's two dozen mature sugar maples. Those trees are irreplaceable and this pavilion will stand in harmony with them, not at their expense. Recreation Committee Chair Owen Lefebvre made the case compellingly at the meeting: the pavilion allows the weekly summer concert series, Heritage Day, and all our other park programming to proceed regardless of weather, and it could extend the concert series by three to four additional dates. In 2024, park events drew an estimated 11,400 visitors. With a covered pavilion, that number will grow. Bids go out in June, groundbreaking is targeted for September, and we aim to have it ready for the 2026 outdoor season.
